title: Integrable systems and toric contact forms on ℝℙ3
speaker: Vinicius Ramos
affiliation: Instituto de Matemática Pura e Aplicada (IMPA)
abstract: It is well-known that the geodesic flow on ellipsoids of revolution is integrable. In joint work with Ferreira and Vicente, we used this fact to obtain a symplectomorphism between the unit disk bundle of such an ellipsoid without fiber and a toric domain. In this talk, I will explain this result and how we can also obtain a symplectomorphism between the whole unit disk bundle and a toric filling of ℝℙ3, which can be concave or convex depending on the original ellipsoid. I will also explain how to generalize this idea to other situations.

proof_stages.label: Toric-domain contact and Reeb calculus
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines toric domains as moment-map preimages, singles out star-shaped/monotone/concave/convex classes, and explains how the Liouville contact form makes the Reeb dynamics readable from normals to the moment image.

proof_stages.label: Hidden toric domains from integrable billiards and geodesic flows
proof_stages.summary: A sequence of prior constructions shows that action-angle methods can turn cotangent disk bundles and Lagrangian products into ordinary toric domains, while disk bundles over S2 reveal a topology problem when a fiber is not removed.

proof_stages.label: RP3 toric domains from the T^*S2 moment cone
proof_stages.summary: To handle the full unit cotangent bundle of S2, whose boundary is RP3 rather than S3, the speaker introduces RP3 toric domains as preimages of regions in the moment cone for the standard T2-action on T^*S2.

proof_stages.label: Arnold-Liouville action-coordinate machine
proof_stages.summary: The proof tool is the Arnold-Liouville theorem: compact connected regular fibers of a commuting Hamiltonian system are tori, and action integrals along smoothly chosen cycles give the local moment coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Realization of ellipsoid and Finsler disk bundles as RP3 toric domains
proof_stages.summary: Using the commuting Hamiltonians given by the S1-invariant norm and angular momentum, disk bundles of ellipsoids of revolution—and, with some in-progress qualifications, S1-invariant Finsler metrics—are identified with explicit RP3 toric domains, concave for c<1 and convex for c>1.

proof_stages.label: Removing a pole fiber to recover ordinary toric domains
proof_stages.summary: Deleting the North or South pole fiber is modeled by a billiard/repulsive-potential limit in the integrable system, producing a usual C2 toric domain via an explicit piecewise-linear transformation and explaining the resulting corner.

proof_stages.label: Lens-space extensions, capacities, and open singularity questions
proof_stages.summary: The same cone-quotient framework is extended to lens-space toric domains, where concave ball packings compute ECH capacities, and the talk closes with questions about convex packings, capacity sharpness, and broader master theorems for integrable systems with more complicated singularities.

examples.name: Very oblate ellipsoid oscillating geodesics
examples.note: For c<1/2 the new simple geodesics oscillating around the equator illustrate how the toric moment image reveals otherwise hard-to-see geodesic-flow and Gromov-width information.
examples.engagement_reason: for very oblate ellipsoids the speaker traces the oscillating geodesics, relates their appearance to slopes in the moment image, defines their lengths, and uses them in a Gromov-width formula
